summary: O'Neil responds to E.O. Wilson's essay arguing scientists don't need to be good at math. She agrees that teamwork between domain experts and quantitative specialists is valuable, but pushes back on Wilson's framing that mathematicians are sitting around waiting for scientists to use their equations. She argues this reflects a common misconception about what mathematicians actually do — they pursue abstract understanding for its own sake, not applied utility. She closes with a warning: scientists who ignore math entirely lose the ability to distinguish genuine mathematical expertise from charlatanism, which can undermine their own research.
key_points: Teamwork between domain experts and quantitative specialists is genuinely valuable — no one person needs all the skills

Wilson's framing reveals a common misconception that mathematicians are tool-providers waiting for scientists to apply their work, when in fact mathematicians pursue abstract understanding for its own sake
Scientists who don't understand math themselves can't distinguish real expertise from charlatanism on their own teams
Shrinking math departments and research funding may force mathematicians toward more applied work in coming years
money_quotes: He is not claiming that math itself is not part of great science, just that he hasn't been the one that supplied the mathy bits.
The cool thing about working on a data science team in industry is that different people bring different skills to the table.
It is a common enough misconception to think of the quant as a guy with a bunch of tools but no understanding or creativity.
Mathematicians, as a group, are not sitting around wishing someone could 'make use of their equations.'

They often think about abstract structures with no goal whatsoever of connecting it back to, say, how ants live in colonies. And that's cool and beautiful too, and it's not a failure of the system. That's just math.

The danger in ignoring the math yourself, if you're a scientist, is that you probably aren't that great at knowing the difference between someone who really knows math and someone who can throw around terminology.
You can't catch charlatans, in other words.
key_insight: Scientists who outsource all mathematical thinking gain the benefits of collaboration but lose the ability to detect when the math on their own team is wrong.
